chunk_summary: Gastrostomy decision-making in neurodisability focuses on shared decision-making, pre-operative optimization, and risk mitigation, addressing complications, anatomical risks, and post-procedure care to improve feeding outcomes and reduce caregiver burden.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of gastrostomy as a long-term feeding strategy in neurodisability.
Identify pre-operative optimization steps to mitigate peri-procedure risks.
Recognize common complications and post-procedure management strategies.
Evaluate anatomical and clinical factors influencing gastrostomy placement.
key_facts: Gastrostomy reduces aspiration risk, improves growth, and shortens feeding time in severe CP cases.
Feeding time >3 hours/day, recurrent pneumonias, and growth faltering are key indicators for gastrostomy.
Pre-operative optimization includes correcting anemia, malnutrition, and constipation, and ensuring respiratory stability.
Antibiotic prophylaxis reduces peristomal infection, and early feeding (within 3 hours) is safe in stable children.
Dislodgement protocols treat the tract as immature for 6-12 weeks, requiring surgical input if dislodged.
High-risk anatomy (e.g., severe scoliosis, ventriculoperitoneal shunt) may necessitate laparoscopic-assisted placement.
reference_quote: Studies in severe CP show improved growth and reduced caregiver feeding time after gastrostomy, but counseling should also cover common minor complications (granulation tissue, leakage, superficial infection).
document_key_topics: Gastrostomy as a long-term feeding strategy
Anatomical and clinical considerations in gastrostomy placement
